title: Research
text: Understanding the population dynamics of solitary bee species remains a considerable challenge for the field. Basic questions such as “how big is a population” or “how does population size fluctuate from year to year” are unknown yet critical for conservation and conducting research. We study these questions along with an emphasis on movement ecology. We investigate the biology of dispersal and foraging across populations and communities and explore which factors affect movement and why.

title: Research
text: Understanding the population dynamics of solitary bee species remains a considerable challenge for the field. Basic questions such as “how big is a population” or “how does population size fluctuate from year to year” are unknown yet critical for conservation and conducting research. We study these questions along with an emphasis on movement ecology. We investigate the biology of dispersal and foraging across populations and communities and explore which factors affect movement and why.

title: Research
text:
Solitary bee nesting studies


Research emphasizes floral resource availability to conserve bees, yet we know so little about nesting requirements of solitary bee species, especially our ground-nesting species that encompass roughly 80% of all bee species. We study all things bee nesting, from nest architecture to phenology and nesting as it relates to dispersal and movement. We are also in the process of getting our environmental chamber up and running in the lab and hope to get some bee nesting action in there. Stay tuned!

title: Research
text:
Effectiveness of bee monitoring


We are interested in exploring different ways to monitor bee communities. Whether this is using non-lethal methods such as mark-release-recapture, targeted surveys, geospatial data, or methods in-between, we want to determine how we can collect data and reduce lethal take.

title: Chase Native Bees Lab
text:
Welcome to the Chase Native Bees Lab! We study all things related to wild native bees, with the broad aim of teasing apart their intricate biologies and ecologies in ways that guide their conservation, as well as restoration and management. Our work centers on bee population dynamics, movement and dispersal, nesting, and natural history — including pollination biology studies on plants bees love! We also conduct broad community-level work using rigorous statistical and geospatial analysis.
      
      Stay tuned as we get going in fall 2026!

title: People
text: The Chase Native Bees Lab is committed to supporting a diverse and inclusive community of scientists from all backgrounds, identities, and career stages. We aim to foster an inclusive and respectful workplace by prioritizing open communication, building supportive relationships, partaking in trainings and workshops, and expanding participation in entomology, the broader biological sciences, and academia.

title: People
text:
Principal Investigator


Marissa Chase — Assistant Professor, Department of Biology, Villanova University
Marissa (she/her) teaches courses in entomology and evolutionary ecology. While her research encompasses all things related to wild bee biology and ecology, with an emphasis on bee population dynamics, nesting, and restoration, she is a bug nerd and lover of all insects. Aside from entomology-related fun, Marissa will happily pet your dog and go on a hike to forage mushrooms or track down a historical rare plant record.

Post-doc — University of Minnesota (adviser: Dan Cariveau)
PhD — University of Illinois (co-advisers: Alex Harmon-Threatt and Jen Fraterrigo)
BS — UC Berkeley

title: People
text:
Staff


Ror — Lab manager
Ror is an exceptional herder, with years of experience herding his mom to lab and making her finish writing her papers. His lab responsibilities include: reminding you to go outside and touch (native) grass and finishing that sandwich you were eating. In his free time, Ror loves long, meandering walks through dumpster-filled alleys, incidental seed dispersal while wading through prairies and muck, and creating bizarre sounds both day and night.
